chunk_summary: Excessive cow’s milk intake in toddlers can suppress appetite for iron-rich foods, increasing anemia risk. Clinical management includes dietary adjustments, anemia testing, and counseling caregivers on balanced complementary feeding practices.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of excessive cow’s milk intake on iron deficiency anemia risk in toddlers.
Learn clinical assessment strategies for anemia-related symptoms like pallor and pica.
Develop dietary intervention plans to reduce milk intake and promote iron-rich complementary feeding.
Recognize the importance of anemia testing and follow-up in suspected cases.
key_facts: Toddlers consuming ~900 mL cow’s milk/day are at risk of iron deficiency anemia due to suppressed appetite for iron-rich foods.
Recommended milk intake for toddlers is ~400–500 mL/day, using an open cup instead of a bottle.
Clinical red flags for anemia include pallor, pica, irritability, and static weight.
Minimum work-up includes CBC with indices (± ferritin), deworming history, and screening for blood loss or chronic diarrhea.
Dietary interventions include nutrient-dense snacks and daily iron-rich foods like egg, meat, fish, or pulses with vitamin C.
reference_quote: High milk volumes suppress appetite for iron-rich family foods and are a common dietary pattern linked to iron deficiency. — CDC Infant and Toddler Nutrition
document_key_topics: Excessive milk intake and anemia risk
Clinical assessment of iron deficiency anemia in toddlers
Dietary interventions for balanced complementary feeding
